LAW & MOTION/DISCOVERY, DEPT. 301, AS TO MAY-23-2025 HEARING RE: DEFENDANT DOORDASH, INC.'S DEMURRER TO AMENDED COMPLAINT; THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. DEFENDANT DOORDASH, INC.'S DEMURRER TO ALL THREE CAUSES OF ACTION IN PLAINTIFF CHIA WEI HSU'S THIRD AMENDED COMPLAINT (TAC) IS SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND AS TO THE FIRST AND SECOND CAUSES OF ACTION FOR UNJUST ENRICHMENT AND QUANTUM MERUIT AND OVERRULED AS TO THE THIRD CAUSE OF ACTION FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT (SEE MINI MINUTES FOR COMPLETE RULING). COUNSEL FOR THE PREVAILING PARTY IS TO PREPARE AND SUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH REPEATS VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE COURT'S RULING WITH A COVER LETTER DEMONSTRATING COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: HAROLD E. KAHN; CLERK: V. DA FONSECA; NOT REPORTED. (301/HEK)

PRO HAC VICE RENEWAL FEE CALENDAR FOR ATTORNEY MICHAEL B. SILVERSTEIN
Off Calendar Jul-22-2025 Inactivate Attorney

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar May-23-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT DOORDASH, INC.'s DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant DoorDash, Inc.'s demurrer to all three causes of action in plaintiff Chia Wei Hsu's third amended complaint (TAC) is sustained without leave to amend as to the first and second causes of action for unjust enrichment and quantum meruit and overruled as to the third cause of action for breach of contract.

Mr. Hsu worked for DoorDash as a software engineer from September 2017 until his termination in March 2019. In late-2019, following his termination, counsel for DoorDash allegedly asked Mr. Hsu to perform work to help DoorDash obtain a patent for a program Mr. Hsu created. Mr. Hsu allegedly provided these services, but was never compensated. DoorDash obtained the patent in 2021. On January 31, 2025, this court sustained DoorDash's demurrer as to Mr. Hsu's claims for unjust enrichment and quantum meruit. The court gave Mr. Hsu leave to amend "to make any good-faith allegations available to him that (1) the PIIA is unenforceable and he may therefore recover under a restitution or quantum meruit theory of recovery; and/or (2) he is entitled to recover under the PIIA." Mr. Hsu's TAC added two paragraphs as to why the PIIA may be unenforceable and added a breach of contract claim for unpaid wages.

The TAC fails to adequately allege that the PIIA is unenforceable. Mr. Hsu argues the agreement is unenforceable because it is unconscionable. Assuming that the PIIA is procedurally unconscionable to some degree, Mr. Hsu has neither alleged or made any persuasive argument the the PIIA has any degree of substantive unconsiconability. Mr. Hsu's allegations that the agreement is unenforceable due to ambiguity and vagueness lack merit. Any missing material terms can be implied by the course of dealing of the parties or other relevant facts and circumstances and do not render the agreement unenforceable.

Nor is the PIIA unenforceable under Labor Code section 2855(a). That section provides that contracts for personal service "may not be enforced against the employee beyond seven years from the commencement of service under it." DoorDash is not seeking to enforce the PIIA beyond seven years after Mr. Hsu's work was commenced under that agreement.

As to the breach of contract claim. Mr. Hsu alleges he performed services for DoorDash and was entitled to compensation. The language of the PIIA ("at Company's expense") is reasonably susceptible to Mr. Hsu's proferred interpretation that he is entitled to compensation for services that he rendered per the PIIA and thus this court may not adjudicate the parties's dispute about the meaning of the contract language on a pleading motion.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling with a cover letter demonstrating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Harold E. Kahn; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/HEK)

Off Calendar Jan-10-2025 Continued To Mar-18-25 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEFENDANT DOORDASH, INC. DEMURRER TO AMENDED COMPLAINT (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2)
Off Calendar Jan-31-2025 Off Calendar

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-31-2025 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT DOORDASH, INC.'s DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's demurrer to the second amended complaint ("SAC") is sustained with 20 days' leave to amend. (CCP 430.10(e).)

The court rejects defendant's statute of limitations argument. Defendant argues that plaintiff's claims accrued on the date plaintiff last rendered a service and citing paragraph 15 of the SAC, that date was May 2, 2020. (E.O.C. Ord, Inc. v. Kovakovich (1988) 200 Cal.App.3d 1194, 1203.) This argument fails for two reasons. First, paragraph 15 of the SAC alleges that plaintiff executed the assignment on May 2, 2020. It is unclear whether that was the last date of service. Second, plaintiff timely filed the original complaint based on Emergency Rule 9(a): "Notwithstanding any other law, the statutes of limitations and repose for civil cause of action that exceed 180 days are tolled from April 6, 2020, until October 1, 2020." Emergency Rule 9 tolled plaintiff's statute of limitations from May 2, 2020, through October 1, 2020. Thus, plaintiff timely filed the original complaint on June 9, 2022.

Next, defendant relies on the Proprietary Information and Inventions Agreement ("PIIA") to argue that this express agreement bars plaintiff's claims for unjust enrichment and quantum meruit. (Sepanoissian v. National Ready Mixed Concrete Co. (2023) 97 Cal.App.5th 192, 207 [unjust enrichment inapplicable where the parties had an enforceable express contract]; Hedging Concepts, Inc. v. First All. Mortg. Co. (1996) 41 Cal.App.4th 1410, 1419 ["[I]t is well settled that there is no equitable basis for an implied-in-law promise to pay reasonable value when the parties have an actual agreement covering compensation."].)

The court will take judicial notice of this agreement, which is attached to the declaration of Justin Steffen filed on August 9, 2022. Plaintiff quotes from the PIIA and does not dispute its authenticity. (Evidence Code section 452(h) [facts not subject to dispute]; Scott v. JPMorgan Chase Bank (2013) 214 Cal.App.4th 743, 746 ["[W]e conclude that the trial court properly took judicial notice of the fact and legal effect of the government's contract with JPMorgan, since Scott did not allege or argue in the trial court that the contract was inauthentic or otherwise reasonably subject to dispute."]; Ingram v. Flippo (1999) 74 Cal.App.4th 1280, 1285, fn.3 [taking judicial notice of a letter that was not attached to the complaint where both parties referred to and quoted from it] (disapproved on other grounds in Leon v. County of Riverside (2023) 14 Cal.5th 910.)

Plaintiff's opposition concedes that the PIIA is enforceable and governs his entitlement to payment. (Opp. Br. 5-6.) In any event the PIIA in Paragraph 8 contemplates that plaintiff's obligations survive the termination of his employment. Plaintiff's current-pleaded claims therefore fail. (See Lance Camper Manufacturing Corp. v. Republic Indemnity Co. (1996) 44 Cal.App.4th 194, 203 ["The Insured must allege that the express contract is void or was rescinded in order to proceed with its quasi-contract claim."].)

Plaintiff has leave to amend to make any good-faith allegations available to him that (1) the PIIA is unenforceable and he may therefore recover under a restitution or quantum meruit theory of recovery; and/or (2) he is entitled to recover under the PIIA

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order To Lift Stay; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Paul V. Bennett, Esq.
Off Calendar Jun-21-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via Zoom:
Paul Vivian Bennett, Esq., Tel. 410.353.4994, for plaintiff Chia Wei Hsu;
Michael B. Silberstein, Tel. 614.223.9362, for defendant Doordash, Inc.

Hearing re: PLAINTIFF CHIA HSU'S Motion For An Order To Lift Stay.

This matter is contested. Argument is held. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiff's motion to lift the stay is granted.

Plaintiff's employment was terminated on March 15, 2019. (Complaint. Par. 1.) Plaintiff subsequently helped defendant with its patent application in December 2019. (Complaint, par. 11.) Plaintiff seeks to recover for that post-employment work. (Complaint, par. 16.)

The court granted defendant's motion to compel arbitration and stay. It also ruled that based on the clear and unmistakable delegation clause, the arbitrator had the authority to rule on the scope of the arbitration. The arbitrator ruled:

Any claim for relief which is based upon the work performed while Hsu worked for DoorDash must be arbitrated as part of this matter and may not be pursued directly in court or any other forum. To the extent Claimant is pursuing a claim for unjust enrichment based in whole or in part upon work he performed while he was an employee, even if coupled with a claim for work after his employment ended, that claim in its entirety must be pursued in this arbitration. Any claim for unjust enrichment based solely upon work performed by Hsu after his employment ended may be pursued in court.

(Bennett Decl., Ex. A, pg. 2)

While there is a connection between plaintiff's pre- and post-termination work, it is clear that in this action, "Claimant seeks relief for unjust enrichment based upon the work he performed on the patent application after Claimant's termination." (Bennett Decl., Ex. B, 2:19-21; see also Complaint, par. 16.) The post-employment claims are therefore severable and it is proper to lift the stay. (Manatt, Phelps, Rothenberg & Tunney v. Lawrence (1984) 151 Cal.App.3d 1165, 1172 [trial court discretion to determine that action is exempt from arbitration; CCP 1281.4 [court discretion to lift stay.)

Order signed.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Matter Not Reported. Bailiff: Loo. (301/RCE)

MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION
Off Calendar Sep-08-2022 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. JAY R. STRAUSS, Esq. of Winton Strauss Law Group, P.C. (Tel: 415-985-2111) for plaintiff;
Atty. NATALIE M. CUADROS, Esq. of Benesch, Friedlander, Coplan & Arnonoff LLP (Tel: 628-600-2250) for defendant Doordash, Inc.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT DOORDASH, INC.'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's motion to compel arbitration and stay is granted.

The parties' arbitration agreement provides "you and the Company agree that any legal dispute or controversy covered by this Agreement, or arising out of, relating to, or concerning the existence, scope, validity, enforceability or breach of this Agreement, shall be resolved by final and binding arbitration in accordance with the Employment Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association ("AAA Rules") then in effect". This delegation clause is clear and unmistakable and therefore, the arbitrator shall decide whether this controversy is arbitrable. (See Rent-A-Center, West, Inc. v. Jackson (2010) 561 U.S. 63, 68-69 ["[P]arties can agree to arbitrate 'gateway' questions of 'arbitrability,' such as whether the parties have agreed to arbitrate or whether their agreement covers a particular controversy."].) In fact, the AAA rules that the parties incorporate by reference also provide that the arbitrator decides arbitrability. (See Employment AAA Rule 6a; Dream Theater, Inc. v. Dream Theater (2004) 124 Cal.App.4th 547 [arbitrator to decide arbitrability because the parties incorporated by reference the AAA rules].) The arbitrator's ruling on the demurrer in the related discrimination claim did not hold that these claims were inarbitrable. Rather, he merely ruled that the post-termination claims were not presently before him. (Strauss Decl., Ex. 1.)

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)
